items.title: Commune of probable infection assignment protocol unspecified pre-Ñuble split
items.evidence: cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío regional office and were assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
items.mechanism: 103/136 (75.7%) cases rely on a reconciliation protocol relegated to Supplementary Methods without stating whether the protocol existed contemporaneously or was applied retrospectively. If retrospective, it introduces recall/records bias. If the Biobío SEREMI used residence (not infection site) pre-2018, systematic spatial misclassification would inflate/deflate commune-specific IRRs in the training period, biasing spatial random effects and FSI coefficients trained on those counts.
items.fix_hint: Move reconciliation protocol to main text Methods (±100 words). State explicitly: (a) whether infection-site geolocation was collected prospectively by Biobío SEREMI 2002-2017 or assigned retrospectively; (b) what fraction of n=103 had field investigation reports vs residence-only records; (c) sensitivity analysis excluding cases with residence≠infection or limiting to post-2018 Ñuble-native registration.
items.mcc_rationale: Prior vault references case reconciliation and probable commune of infection protocol, but no Q1 citation provided, no numeric calculation shown, and no explicit integrated sentence in manuscript.

items.title: FSI wildfire-veto threshold (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) lacks calibration evidence
items.evidence: with active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination (Ecke et al. 2019)
items.mechanism: The ΔNBR2 = −0.15 threshold is cited to Ecke 2019 (a Scandinavian rodent study), but Ñuble's Mediterranean-climate wildfires have different burn severity distributions. If the threshold is too liberal, undetected fire scars remain in FSI, creating spurious negative correlations (burned forest → low FSI → high apparent risk). If too strict, it masks true C. quila post-fire regrowth signals. No sensitivity analysis across thresholds [−0.10, −0.20] is reported.
items.fix_hint: Report fire-pixel prevalence in the Ñuble FSI series (% months affected). Perform sensitivity analysis varying ΔNBR2 ∈ {−0.10, −0.12, −0.15, −0.18, −0.20} and show IRR_R5 stability. If IRR sign flips, this undermines the ecological interpretation; if stable, cite Ñuble-specific justification or mark as exploratory calibration.
items.mcc_rationale: Ecke 2019 citation present, ΔNBR2 threshold −0.15 documented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51.md and project memory S51, but no explicit calibration validation or integrated sentence in manuscript.

items.title: Achibueno FSI calibration site selection bias (single-watershed pre-freeze)
items.evidence: calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble (anti-circularity)
items.mechanism: Achibueno is cited as the external calibration watershed, but no rationale for its selection is given. If Achibueno was chosen post-hoc because it had known hantavirus cases or visible C. quila signals, the 'anti-circularity' claim is undermined by ex post site selection. The FSI formula (NDMI+NDVI+NBR2 weights) derived from Achibueno may not generalize to Ñuble's coastal vs interior quiloide ecotones (Ward zones), introducing spatial transferability bias.
items.fix_hint: State Achibueno selection criteria (geographic proximity? independent case data? literature precedent?) and show FSI correlation with known C. quila flowering events in that watershed. Perform split-sample validation: train FSI weights in Achibueno, test in a held-out Ñuble commune subset, report out-of-sample IRR_R5 to confirm transferability before full-panel application.
items.mcc_rationale: Q1 anti-circularity protocol documented in Estado_Conocimiento_Serie_Ecologica_S50.md, Achibueno-freeze procedure explicitly described in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51.md, integrated in manuscript.

items.title: CONAF seasonal patrol coverage (Oct-Apr) confounds operational risk window
items.evidence: CONAF patrol coverage is seasonal (October–April), only partially overlapping the primary risk window
items.mechanism: CONAF's seasonal patrol schedule (Oct–Apr) aligns with the HCPS seasonal peak (91.2% cases Oct–May). If CONAF access restrictions are only enforced during patrol months, observed case reductions in that window could reflect patrol-driven ascertainment bias (reduced access → fewer infected individuals → fewer diagnosed cases) rather than true ecological risk reduction. This confounds the FSI→risk pathway and inflates apparent model skill during validation folds that include Oct–Apr months.
items.fix_hint: Test for differential FSI effect by patrol season: fit an interaction term FSI_R5 × I(month ∈ Oct–Apr). If IRR differs significantly, stratify skill metrics by patrol vs non-patrol months. Alternatively, limit operational claims to year-round municipal-SEREMI coordination and note CONAF seasonality as a coverage limitation, not a validation threat.
items.mcc_rationale: CONAF Oct-Apr patrol coverage documented in Auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre.md, overlap with risk window explicitly noted in Paper_EID_Final.md, integrated in manuscript limitation section.

items.title: 87% peridomestic transmission assumption (Riquelme 2015) unverified in Ñuble
items.evidence: 87% peridomestic transmission per Riquelme 2015 supports spatial accuracy
items.mechanism: Riquelme 2015 is cited to justify commune-level spatial accuracy under the infection-site assignment protocol, but the 87% figure likely derives from national-level or different-region data (not Ñuble-specific 2002–2024). Ñuble has distinct occupational patterns (forestry, agriculture) and settlement density; if the true peridomestic fraction is lower (e.g., 60–70% due to higher sylvatic/occupational exposure), commune-of-infection assignment loses precision, increasing non-differential misclassification and attenuating IRR_R5 toward the null.
items.fix_hint: Report Ñuble-specific peridomestic fraction from field investigation records (if available) or sensitivity analysis: simulate misclassification under 60%, 75%, 87% peridomestic scenarios, re-estimate IRR_R5, and show CI overlap. If unavailable, downgrade the '87% supports accuracy' claim to 'national data suggest majority peridomestic; residual misclassification expected to bias toward null.'
items.mcc_rationale: Riquelme 2015 citation present, 87% peridomestic transmission documented in multiple vault files, but no Ñuble-specific validation calculation or integrated caveat sentence in manuscript.

items.title: Case reconciliation protocol in Supplementary Methods creates verifiability barrier
items.evidence: following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
items.mechanism: The protocol governing 75.7% of cases (103/136 pre-Ñuble split) is not in the main text. Reviewers and readers cannot assess whether infection-site assignment was prospective (high validity) or retrospective chart review (subject to recall bias, missing geolocation data, residence-infection conflation). This is an information bias: the measurement process is described, but its quality is unverifiable without accessing supplementary materials that may not be peer-reviewed to the same standard.
items.fix_hint: Promote the reconciliation protocol summary to main Methods (5.5.1), including: (a) data source (field reports vs medical records vs SEREMI administrative logs), (b) geolocation completeness (% with GPS coordinates vs residence proxy), (c) inter-rater reliability if retrospective assignment was performed. Reserve detailed flowcharts for Supplementary, but ensure core validity metrics are in main text.
items.mcc_rationale: Case reconciliation protocol referenced in vault Panel Oficial M1M2.md and Estado_Conocimiento_Serie_Ecologica_S50.md, but no Q1 citation for reconciliation method and no integrated phrase.

items.title: Commune aggregation masks within-commune quiloide heterogeneity
items.evidence: No inference is at the comuna–month panel level... Ecological fallacy (comuna-month aggregation carries inherent individual-level inference limits per Robinson 1950; Morgenstern 1995)
items.mechanism: Ñuble communes vary 10–100× in area; within large communes (e.g., Coihueco 1,153 km²), C. quila patches are spatially heterogeneous. The FSI is spatially averaged across the entire commune, diluting focal quiloide signals. If a 10 km² high-risk quiloide patch exists within a 1,000 km² commune, the FSI signal is attenuated 100-fold, biasing IRR_R5 toward the null and underestimating true ecological associations.
items.fix_hint: Perform sensitivity analysis at sub-commune resolution: (a) mask FSI to only forested pixels >800 m elevation (quiloide zone proxy); (b) compute 90th-percentile FSI within each commune instead of mean; (c) re-estimate IRR_R5 and compare CI width. If IRR strengthens materially (e.g., from 0.73 to 0.55), the averaging dilution is substantial and should be noted as a conservative bias.
items.mcc_rationale: Robinson 1950 and Morgenstern 1995 citations present, ecological fallacy explicitly discussed in GLMM NB2 Final.md and Blindajes G1-G6.md, integrated in manuscript limitations.

items.title: Companion clinical study ethics approval overlap creates selective reporting risk
items.evidence: both manuscripts were designed as complementary analyses under ICMJE criteria for acceptable secondary publication, with full disclosure to both journals... share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics; only publicly available regional descriptors (total case count, case-fatality ratio, ethics approval) are common
items.mechanism: The companion clinical study (Contreras et al., Rev Chil Infect) analyzes 34 HCPS patients 2010–2025, a subset of the 136 ecological cases. If negative ecological findings (e.g., non-significant lags, unexpected IRR signs) were selectively partitioned into the clinical paper to preserve the EWS narrative, this constitutes outcome reporting bias. The 'no shared metrics' claim prevents verification of whether all pre-specified ecological analyses appear in this manuscript or were moved elsewhere.
items.fix_hint: In Supplementary Methods, list all pre-specified analyses from Zenodo v1.0/v1.1/v1.2 and cross-reference where each appears (EID ms vs RCI ms vs 'not pursued'). If any lag profiles, spatial analyses, or sensitivity tests were computed but not reported here, state the disposition (e.g., 'lag 0–4 and 6–10 reported in Supplementary Fig S2; clinical subgroup analyses in companion RCI manuscript').
items.mcc_rationale: ICMJE secondary publication criteria documented in project_dual_manuscript_factory_S44.md, complementary analysis statement integrated in Paper_EID_Final.md and Manuscrito_v5_CONDENSED_S60.md.

items.title: PAHO 2025 alert citation in Conclusions lacks methodological connection
items.evidence: Against the regional backdrop of the PAHO December 2025 epidemiological alert documenting renewed hantavirus activity across the Americas (PAHO 2025), the work supports adoption...
items.mechanism: The PAHO 2025 alert is invoked to frame urgency but appears only in the final sentence of Conclusions, without analysis of whether the 'renewed activity' is detectable in Ñuble's 2024 data or validation folds. If the alert reflects outbreaks in other countries (Argentina, Bolivia) with different hantavirus species (e.g., Laguna Negra) or ecology (Chaco vs Andean), citing it creates false relevance bias—implying the Ñuble EWS generalizes to PAHO-wide contexts without empirical support.
items.fix_hint: Either (a) analyze whether Ñuble 2023–2024 case counts are elevated vs the Bortman threshold and explicitly link this to the PAHO alert, or (b) remove the PAHO citation from Conclusions and place it in Introduction/Discussion as context for regional surveillance gaps, without claiming the current model addresses PAHO-wide needs.
items.mcc_rationale: PAHO 2025 citation present, multiple vault references to Manuscrito_v5_CONDENSED_S60.md, but no methodological connection calculation and no explicit sentence linking alert to study design.

items.title: Zone factor dropped for 'redundancy' without testing zone×FSI interaction
items.evidence: Zone factor was dropped for redundancy with the comuna random intercept (LRT p = 0.186; Hodges & Reich 2010)
items.mechanism: The manuscript states zone (coastal vs interior quiloide binary) was dropped due to LRT p = 0.186, but does not test whether zone moderates the FSI effect (zone × FSI_R5 interaction). If coastal communes have different C. quila phenology (maritime influence) vs interior Andean foothills, the IRR_R5 could differ by zone. Dropping the main effect does not rule out effect modification, and the final model assumes FSI operates identically across both ecotones.
items.fix_hint: Fit a sensitivity model including zone × FSI_R5 interaction and report the LRT p-value. If p > 0.10, the no-interaction assumption is supported. If p < 0.10, report stratum-specific IRRs (coastal vs interior) and discuss whether the single-IRR interpretation holds or requires geographic qualification.
items.mcc_rationale: Hodges & Reich 2010 citation present, LRT p=0.186 documented in GLMM NB2 Final.md and Paper_EID_Final.md, zone-factor redundancy explicitly stated in manuscript model selection section.

items.title: Case definition change IgM→RT-PCR without sensitivity/specificity adjustment
items.evidence: case definition: IgM seroconversion or RT-PCR positive per MINSAL circular B51/24
items.mechanism: IgM-based diagnosis (pre-2012) has different sensitivity/specificity than RT-PCR (post-2012), potentially creating artificial temporal trends in case ascertainment independent of true incidence. No adjustment for diagnostic test performance applied.
items.fix_hint: Conduct sensitivity analysis weighting pre/post-2012 cases by known IgM vs RT-PCR sensitivity/specificity ratios, or stratify walk-forward folds by diagnostic era
items.mcc_rationale: Case definition IgM/RT-PCR documented in official panel metadata and manuscript v5, but no Q1 citation addressing sensitivity/specificity trade-offs of the 2024 diagnostic switch.

items.title: Commune of probable infection assignment lacks validation
items.evidence: assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
items.mechanism: No validation data provided for infection-vs-residence assignment accuracy. If misclassification rate >13% (given 87% peridomestic claim), spatial mismatch between predictor (commune-level FSI) and outcome (mis-assigned cases) introduces non-differential exposure misclassification, biasing IRR toward null unpredictably.
items.fix_hint: Report inter-rater reliability for infection commune assignment or conduct probabilistic sensitivity analysis across plausible misclassification rates (10-30%)
items.mcc_rationale: Commune-of-infection assignment protocol mentioned in manuscript v5 and Supp Methods, but no validation statistics or Q1 citation quantifying misclassification rate.

items.title: Rodent density proxy absent despite being causal mediator
items.evidence: O. longicaudatus transmits the virus... subliminal two-to-fivefold rise in O. longicaudatus density — a phenomenon we term 'ratización'
items.mechanism: FSI → rodent density → human cases is the stated causal path, yet no rodent abundance measurement appears in the model. Without this mediator, FSI coefficient conflates direct effects with unmeasured rodent population dynamics, potentially invalidating the ecological interpretation of ψ_R5.
items.fix_hint: Add historical trapping data as sensitivity check, or explicitly state FSI is an instrumental variable with assumptions required for valid inference
items.mcc_rationale: Ratización concept and O. longicaudatus causal role documented in hypothesis files and Estado_Conocimiento S50, but no direct rodent density covariate in final GLMM model.

items.title: Wildfire veto threshold (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) lacks empirical justification
items.evidence: active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination
items.mechanism: Arbitrary threshold without validation against known fire perimeters. If threshold is too lenient, post-fire vegetation recovery (which may attract rodents) is misclassified as forest stress; if too strict, legitimate stress signals are censored, biasing FSI and attenuating ψ_R5 unpredictably.
items.fix_hint: Validate threshold against CONAF fire perimeter database or conduct sensitivity analysis across ΔNBR2 ∈ [−0.10, −0.20]
items.mcc_rationale: dNBR2 < -0.15 threshold implemented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion S51 scripts, but no peer-reviewed citation or empirical validation justifying -0.15 cutpoint for bamboo ecosystems.

items.title: Negative ψ_R5 interpretation reversal unreported in pre-specification
items.evidence: The negative ψ reflects R as a post-flowering scar signal: high R means the C. quila cycle has already peaked
items.mechanism: Pre-specification likely assumed positive association (high stress → high cases). Post-hoc reinterpretation of negative coefficient as 'post-flowering scar' without documented a priori hypothesis risks narrative HARKing. If pre-spec v1.0 stated positive direction, sign reversal should trigger sensitivity checks.
items.fix_hint: Publish pre-specification v1.0 directional hypothesis for ψ_R5; if reversed, report as exploratory finding requiring external validation
items.mcc_rationale: Negative ψ_R5 interpretation present in decision_opcionA S50 and Paper_EID_Final, but pre-specification doc lacks explicit HARKing disclosure of sign reversal discovery.

items.title: Companion clinical study selective outcome reporting risk
items.evidence: A companion clinical analysis... has been submitted separately... share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics
items.mechanism: If companion study failed to find prognostic associations, selective non-publication of null results while publishing positive ecological findings inflates apparent hantavirus predictability literature-wide. No trial registration or pre-specification mentioned for clinical study.
items.fix_hint: Register companion clinical study retrospectively at ClinicalTrials.gov or state explicitly if negative findings will be published regardless of significance
items.mcc_rationale: Dual-publication strategy documented in estrategia_dual S42, independence sentence in v5 manuscript, and Parte II master file confirms zero overlap. PREPRINT status mitigates outcome-reporting risk.

items.title: 87% peridomestic transmission citation (Riquelme 2015) location mismatch
items.evidence: 87% peridomestic transmission per Riquelme 2015 supports spatial accuracy
items.mechanism: If Riquelme 2015 study was conducted outside Ñuble region or in different ecological zone, applying its 87% estimate to justify Ñuble commune-level assignment creates cross-population transportability error. Peridomestic fraction may differ in quiloide vs non-quiloide zones.
items.fix_hint: Verify Riquelme 2015 study region; if non-Ñuble, conduct local chart review subsample to estimate Ñuble-specific peridomestic fraction
items.mcc_rationale: Riquelme 2015 87% peridomestic figure cited in Auditoria_Q1_S50 and Paper_EID_Final, but no explicit geographic concordance check between Riquelme cohort and Ñuble study area.

items.title: FSI composite formula weights frozen in Achibueno without Ñuble validation
items.evidence: custom Forest Stress Index (FSI) derived from... NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands... calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble
items.mechanism: Achibueno and Ñuble differ in elevation, forest composition, and precipitation regime. FSI weights optimized for Achibueno may mis-weight spectral bands in Ñuble's quiloide patches, introducing systematic measurement error in the primary exposure variable.
items.fix_hint: Report sensitivity analysis with equal-weight FSI or validate current weights against field-sampled Ñuble C. quila patches
items.mcc_rationale: FSI Achibueno calibration documented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion S51 and Estado_Conocimiento S50, but no Ñuble-specific validation analysis quantifying out-of-sample prediction error.

items.title: CONAF seasonal patrol coverage (Oct-Apr) incomplete over risk window
items.evidence: CONAF patrol coverage is seasonal (October–April), only partially overlapping the primary risk window
items.mechanism: If CONAF detects quiloide flowering only during Oct-Apr, but cases occur year-round (including lag-5 from May-Sep flowering), off-season flowering events are systematically under-detected in FSI, creating seasonally varying exposure measurement error that confounds the lag-5 estimate.
items.fix_hint: Report monthly FSI availability completeness or restrict analysis to Oct-Apr detection windows with explicit seasonal generalizability caveat
items.mcc_rationale: CONAF seasonal Oct-Apr coverage explicitly stated in Auditoria_Q1_S50 cierre and Paper_EID_Final Methods. Temporal mismatch acknowledged as limitation in manuscript Discussion section.

items.title: Zero September cases structural assumption untested
items.evidence: 91.2% occurred October–May, with zero cases in September across 23 consecutive years
items.mechanism: 23-year zero count in September likely reflects surveillance artifact (end-of-winter reporting lag) or true biological null period. If artifact, model's season_f factor absorbs spurious September effect; if biological, treating as fixed structural zero ignores rare-event tail risk (e.g., climate-shifted phenology).
items.fix_hint: Test whether September zero-inflation is surveillance artifact via case-onset-vs-report-date analysis, or model as overdispersed Poisson rather than structural zero
items.mcc_rationale: Zero September cases across 23 years documented in Panel Oficial M1M2 and Estado_Conocimiento S50, but no formal statistical test ruling out random chance or structural break.

items.title: INE intercensal log-linear interpolation ignores migration shocks
items.evidence: INE population estimates with log-linear intercensal interpolation
items.mechanism: Log-linear interpolation assumes smooth exponential growth between censuses, missing abrupt migration events (e.g., 2010 earthquake, 2017 Ñuble administrative split). If population denominators are mis-estimated during shock years, incidence rate standardization introduces time-varying bias.
items.fix_hint: Use INE's official annual projections if available, or flag census years as potential structural break points in sensitivity analysis
items.mcc_rationale: No prior evidence found in memory/, obsidian_vault/, or audit/ regarding INE log-linear interpolation methodology or migration shock limitations. Genuinely new methodological critique.

items.title: Forest cover fraction static despite 22-year study period
items.evidence: static covariates (forest cover fraction, elevation, slope)
items.mechanism: Forest cover changed 2002-2024 due to fires, logging, and CONAF reforestation. Using time-invariant baseline forest cover misses temporal confounding: communes with increasing cover may have rising rodent habitat independently of FSI, biasing spatial between-commune effects.
items.fix_hint: Derive annual forest cover from Landsat time series or include linear forest-cover trend as sensitivity covariate
items.mcc_rationale: Prior refs confirm static covariates (forest, elevation, slope) are documented in methods across multiple files, but no Q1 citation justifying 22-year static assumption nor numeric sensitivity analysis.

items.title: Companion clinical manuscript creates selective outcome reporting risk
items.evidence: A companion clinical analysis of 34 HCPS patients treated at Hospital Clínico Herminda Martín, Chillán, 2010–2025 has been submitted separately...share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics; only publicly available regional descriptors (total case count, case-fatality ratio, ethics approval) are common
items.mechanism: Separate submission of clinical (n=34, 2010-2025) and ecological (n=136, 2002-2024) analyses from overlapping case populations raises selective reporting concern. If clinical manuscript reports null or contradictory findings for temporal patterns, readers lack cross-validation. Disclosure states 'no shared metrics' but does not address overlapping case inclusion or outcome definitions.
items.fix_hint: Provide cross-tabulation of case overlap (how many of 34 clinical cases appear in 136 ecological cases); pre-register companion analysis jointly or explain why clinical temporal patterns were not incorporated as ecological predictors.
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence in project_estrategia_dual_publication_S42.md documents dual-publication strategy, separation of clinical/eco-epi manuscripts, and selective reporting mitigation already integrated into Methods.

items.title: FSI lag-5 structural missingness (105 obs, 1.8%) creates unbalanced panel
items.evidence: FSI lag-5 was structurally unavailable for the first five months of each commune's series (105 observations, 1.8%, excluded)
items.mechanism: First 5 months of each commune (21 communes × 5 months = 105 obs) systematically excluded from analysis. If early-year observations differ systematically (e.g., seasonal transition effects, New Year administrative patterns), listwise deletion creates temporal imbalance. Small fraction (1.8%) limits material impact but could interact with September structural zero.
items.fix_hint: Report whether excluded 105 observations include any non-zero cases; if yes, conduct sensitivity analysis imputing lag-5 FSI via seasonal climatology to retain temporal balance.
items.mcc_rationale: FSI lag-5 structural missingness (105 obs, 1.8%) documented in project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md with explicit panel-balancing strategy and Methods sentence already integrated.

items.title: Case-fatality ratio 27.9% computed on same 136 cases used for forecasting
items.evidence: Case-fatality reached 27.9% (38/136; Wilson 95% CI 21.1–36.0%)
items.mechanism: Case-fatality is outcome-conditional statistic (deaths|cases) computed on same cases used for incidence forecasting. If high-CFR cases cluster temporally or spatially due to shared risk factors (e.g., delayed treatment in remote communes), CFR estimate is not independent of forecasting covariates. Minor concern given CFR reported as descriptive context, not model input.
items.fix_hint: Stratify CFR by high-FSI vs low-FSI commune-months to test whether environmental risk predicts not just incidence but also severity; if no association, reinforces independence assumption.
items.mcc_rationale: CFR 27.9% (38/136, Wilson CI 21.1–36.0%) computed in project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md, cited in Estado_Conocimiento_Serie_Ecologica_S50.md, and integrated into Results per feedback_ci_obligatorio_Nbajo.md.

items.title: Commune-of-infection assignment protocol undocumented in main text
items.evidence: cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío regional office and were assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—**following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods**
items.mechanism: Main text relegates critical spatial exposure assignment (103/136 = 75.7% of cases) to supplement without summarizing criteria. If reconciliation protocol used residence as proxy when infection locale was ambiguous, or lacked standardized criteria, spatial misclassification could bias FSI lag estimates toward null or create spurious associations in high-mobility border communes
items.fix_hint: Summarize reconciliation protocol criteria in main Methods (e.g., 'infection commune assigned via epidemiological interview when available [n=X], residence when interview incomplete [n=Y], or excluded when ambiguous [n=Z]') with sensitivity analysis excluding ambiguous assignments
items.mcc_rationale: Main text references Supplementary Methods for protocol but no Q1 citation or explicit calculation visible in prior evidence. Grep returned empty snippets.

items.title: ΔNBR2 < −0.15 wildfire veto threshold lacks empirical justification
items.evidence: active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination (Ecke et al. 2019)
items.mechanism: Ecke 2019 studied Swedish rodent outbreaks post-fire, not Chilean Chusquea flowering. The −0.15 threshold is stated without validation against known Ñuble wildfire scars (2017 mega-fires affected quiloide zones). If threshold misclassifies post-fire regeneration as flowering signal, FSI becomes confounded by fire ecology; if too conservative, loses true flowering pixels
items.fix_hint: Validate threshold against known 2017 Ñuble fire perimeters (CONAF registry). Show ΔNBR2 distribution for quiloide pixels inside vs outside fire scars. Perform sensitivity analysis with ±0.05 threshold variants
items.mcc_rationale: Prior vault references GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51.md and Ecke 2019 citation exists, but no empirical justification of -0.15 threshold calculation found in snippets.

items.title: Peridomestic transmission assumption (87%) not verified in study sample
items.evidence: 87% peridomestic transmission per Riquelme 2015 supports spatial accuracy; residual misclassification biases estimates toward null
items.mechanism: Riquelme 2015 source not in manuscript (likely national aggregate). If Ñuble's transmission pattern differs (e.g., higher occupational silviculture exposure in El Carmen/Pinto with forest-industry economies), the commune-level FSI→case link weakens. Assuming 87% peridomestic in a potentially occupational-dominant region creates confounding by work-related forest exposure unrelated to rodent habitat dynamics
items.fix_hint: Stratify Ñuble cases by exposure setting from SEREMI records (peridomestic vs occupational vs recreational). Report stratum-specific IRR for FSI. If occupational >30%, add forest-worker density covariate or restrict to peridomestic-only subset
items.mcc_rationale: Riquelme 2015 Q1 citation present in prior memory (project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md), but no empirical verification of 87% in Ñuble study sample found.

items.title: Structural September zero conflated with seasonal factor
items.evidence: 91.2% occurred October–May, with zero cases in September across 23 consecutive years
items.mechanism: Perfect September zero (0/23 years) suggests biological floor (pre-dispersal lag), but season_f 4-level factor treats September identically to other low-risk months. If September is mechanistically different (too early in rodent cycle), pooling it with June-August dilutes seasonal contrast and may bias lag estimates if flowering-to-dispersal window excludes September by design
items.fix_hint: Create 5-level season factor isolating September, or exclude September observations from training as structurally uninformative. Test whether September coefficient differs significantly from other winter months
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence confirms 91.2% October–May seasonality pattern and September structural zero, but no explicit sentence integrated in manuscript describing conflation risk.

items.title: FSI compound weights (NDMI+NDVI+NBR2) frozen in Achibueno without Ñuble empirical validation
items.evidence: FSI derived from Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands via Google Earth Engine...calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble (anti-circularity)
items.mechanism: Achibueno (Maule, different bamboo density/species mix per Veblen 1982) may have different spectral signatures for flowering vs drought stress. Freezing weights without Ñuble ground-truth creates measurement error if Ñuble quiloide reflectance differs. Already flagged as S59-B1-03 for undocumented weights, but external-calibration risk is distinct: transferability assumption untested
items.fix_hint: Report correlation between Achibueno-calibrated FSI and field-observed Ñuble flowering events (if available from CONAF records). If correlation <0.6, recalibrate weights using Ñuble-specific training data with proper held-out validation
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence documents Achibueno calibration and anti-circularity protocol (project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md), but lacks Ñuble-specific empirical validation calculation.

items.title: Companion clinical manuscript creates selective outcome reporting risk
items.evidence: A companion clinical analysis of 34 HCPS patients...submitted separately (Contreras et al., Revista Chilena de Infectología)...share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics; only publicly available regional descriptors (total case count, case-fatality ratio, ethics approval) are common
items.mechanism: Even with claimed separation, if clinical manuscript reports individual-level covariates (e.g., rural residence, occupation) that correlate with ecological FSI, reviewers/readers cannot assess whether ecological model would hold after individual-level adjustment. Splitting complementary analyses into different journals creates publication bias risk if one manuscript is rejected and findings are not integrated
items.fix_hint: State whether companion manuscript will be cross-referenced if both accepted. Deposit individual-level de-identified covariates (age, sex, rural/urban, occupation category) in Zenodo supplement to enable future ecological fallacy assessment
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence (project_estrategia_dual_publication_S42.md) contains explicit dual-publication strategy, Contreras citation, and manuscript separation sentence already integrated.

items.title: Forest cover fraction static covariate not interacted with FSI
items.evidence: static covariates (forest cover fraction, elevation, slope)...zone factor was dropped for redundancy with the comuna random intercept
items.mechanism: FSI signal should be stronger in high-forest-cover communes (more quiloide habitat). No FSI×forest_cover interaction means model assumes FSI slope is identical in 10% vs 90% forested areas. If FSI effect is concentrated in forest-rich communes, pooled estimate is attenuated and precision is overstated
items.fix_hint: Test FSI×forest_cover_fraction interaction via LRT. If p<0.10, stratify by forest tertile and report stratum-specific IRRs. If interaction is significant, add to DAG as effect modifier
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence shows static covariate inclusion (GLMM NB2 Final.md) and zone redundancy drop, but no explicit manuscript sentence explaining non-interaction with FSI.

items.title: Case definition switch (IgM pre-2012 vs RT-PCR post-2012) temporal trend not quantified
items.evidence: case definition: IgM seroconversion or RT-PCR positive per MINSAL circular B51/24...already flagged S59-B1-01 for drift
items.mechanism: Pre/post-2012 incidence ratio unadjusted for diagnostics creates temporal confounding with satellite platform transitions
items.fix_hint: Add binary pre/post-2012 covariate to model. Report adjusted IRR and test whether FSI coefficient changes >20% when included. If IRR≠1, conduct sensitivity analysis restricted to 2013-2024 (homogeneous RT-PCR era)
items.mcc_rationale: MINSAL circular B51/24 cited and S59-B1-01 drift flag exists in prior evidence, but no quantified temporal trend calculation (e.g., pre/post-2012 rate ratio) found.

items.title: Log-linear population offset forces proportional incidence assumption
items.evidence: β_pop · log(pop_it)...β_log_pop = +0.614 (p = 0.002)
items.mechanism: Offset with coefficient = 1 forces incidence rate; estimated β_pop = 0.614 ≠ 1.0 suggests sub-linear scaling (larger communes have lower per-capita risk). Model includes log(pop) as covariate rather than offset, which is correct, but labeling as 'offset' in formula is misleading. If reader implements as true offset, forces incorrect proportionality assumption
items.fix_hint: Clarify that log(pop) enters as covariate with estimated slope, not offset. Report LRT comparing free β_pop vs constrained β_pop=1.0 to show offset is rejected. Label as 'population covariate' not 'offset' in formula notation
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence includes β_log_pop = +0.614 (p=0.002) from GLMM NB2 Final.md, proportional incidence assumption cited (Bell & Jones 2015), and sentence in manuscript.

items.title: Within-between decomposition attribution without individual-level validation
items.evidence: Within–between decomposition (Bell & Jones 2015) ruled out spatial confounding (ψ_between = −0.018, p = 0.935; Hausman p = 0.291)
items.mechanism: Bell & Jones method separates within-commune temporal variation from between-commune spatial differences, but cannot distinguish whether within-commune FSI effect is causal or confounded by unmeasured time-varying factors (e.g., changing CONAF patrol intensity, rural electrification reducing rodent habitat). Hausman test only detects fixed vs random intercept inconsistency, not unmeasured confounding
items.fix_hint: Acknowledge that within-estimator removes time-invariant confounding but not time-varying confounders. List plausible time-varying confounders (rural development index, CONAF budget) and report whether data exist to test them
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence shows Bell & Jones 2015 Q1 citation, within-between decomposition ψ_between=-0.018 p=0.935 and Hausman p=0.291 calculations, and explicit Methods sentence.

items.title: Commune of probable infection vs residence classification without validation
items.evidence: cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío regional office and were assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
items.mechanism: Retrospective assignment of 103/136 cases (76%) to probable infection commune introduces misclassification bias. If assignment criteria differ systematically by commune characteristics (e.g., rural vs urban, forest cover), spatial risk estimates and FSI associations could be artifactually inflated or dampened. The 87% peridomestic transmission cited elsewhere doesn't validate the spatial assignment accuracy.
items.fix_hint: Report sensitivity analysis comparing results using (1) residence commune, (2) infection commune, (3) cases with documented infection location only. Quantify inter-rater reliability if manual adjudication was used. Report proportion of cases with definitive vs probable spatial assignment.
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence documents the SEREMI registration issue and n=103 pre-regionalization cases in multiple locations, but no Q1 citation validates the commune-of-infection assignment protocol nor explicit calculation of misclassification rate.

items.title: FSI compound index weights and validation undocumented
items.evidence: a custom Forest Stress Index (FSI) derived from Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands via Google Earth Engine...calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble
items.mechanism: FSI is the primary exposure but the manuscript provides no formula, band weights, or validation metrics. 'Calibrated in Achibueno' suggests optimization on a different region without documented transferability assessment. Readers cannot reproduce the exposure or assess whether weights capitalize on chance associations in the calibration sample.
items.fix_hint: Report exact FSI formula with band weights. Provide validation metrics (correlation with field-measured Chusquea flowering/rodent density) from Achibueno and Ñuble independently. Report band contribution variance decomposition. Archive GEE script with version-locked band definitions.
items.mcc_rationale: FSI calibration in Achibueno and GEE pipeline documented across multiple sessions (S51, S54, S59), but no Q1 citation for the specific NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 weighting scheme nor validation statistics integrated in manuscript Methods.

items.title: No control for temporal trends in surveillance intensity post-regionalization
items.evidence: Ñuble was administratively established as an independent region by Law 21,033 of 5 September 2017, effective 6 September 2018; cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío
items.mechanism: Administrative split creating a new regional SEREMI office typically increases local diagnostic capacity, case investigation resources, and reporting completeness. The 2018 regionalization creates a natural experiment where post-2018 cases may have higher ascertainment than pre-2018 cases under the same ecological conditions, confounding the FSI association with secular surveillance trends.
items.fix_hint: Include a binary covariate (pre/post-2018 regionalization) or spline term in sensitivity analysis. Compare diagnostic test volumes (IgM, RT-PCR) and case investigation completeness metrics before/after split. Test interaction between FSI and period.
items.mcc_rationale: Regionalization date and n=103 pre-2018 cases documented in Estado_Conocimiento and Auditoria_Q1_S50, but no temporal-trend sensitivity analysis nor Q1 citation for surveillance-intensity adjustment methodology.

items.title: Active-burn veto threshold arbitrary without sensitivity analysis
items.evidence: active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination
items.mechanism: The ΔNBR2 < -0.15 threshold determines which pixels are excluded as wildfire scars. If threshold is too permissive, fire scars contaminate FSI (spurious positive signal); if too stringent, legitimate forest stress is vetoed (attenuated signal). No justification or sensitivity analysis provided for this critical decision boundary.
items.fix_hint: Report sensitivity of FSI-case association (IRR and CI) across ΔNBR2 thresholds {-0.10, -0.15, -0.20, -0.25}. Cite literature justifying -0.15 cutpoint for temperate Nothofagus-Chusquea forests specifically. Report proportion of pixels vetoed at each threshold.
items.mcc_rationale: ΔNBR2 < −0.15 threshold mentioned in Estado_Conocimiento and Sesion_Code_S43, but no Q1 citation justifying the cutoff nor sensitivity analysis (±0.05, ±0.10) reported in manuscript.

items.title: Bell-Jones within-between decomposition applied after lag structure creates NA
items.evidence: All continuous predictors were Bell–Jones within–between decomposed (Bell & Jones 2015), with scaling after NA filtering of the lag structure (anti-leakage)
items.mechanism: Lagging FSI by 5 months creates 105 structural NAs (1.8%) at the start of each commune series. If within-between decomposition is computed on the lag-complete subset only, the 'between' commune means exclude the first 5 months systematically—potentially biasing the between component if early-period values differ (e.g., different forest conditions 2002-2003 vs later periods).
items.fix_hint: Compute commune-specific means for Bell-Jones decomposition using all available data before lagging, then apply lag structure. Report sensitivity comparing (1) means from full series vs (2) means from lag-complete subset. Verify temporal stationarity of FSI within communes.
items.mcc_rationale: Bell & Jones 2015 within-between decomposition cited in Manuscrito_v5_CONDENSED_S60 and Estado_Conocimiento_S50, but no explicit calculation documenting NA-removal order (lag first vs decompose first) nor anti-leakage validation.

items.title: Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 sensor harmonization undocumented
items.evidence: a custom Forest Stress Index (FSI) derived from Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands
items.mechanism: Four satellite platforms with different spectral response functions, radiometric resolution, and temporal coverage are combined into a single index. Without documented cross-calibration, temporal trends in FSI could reflect sensor transitions rather than ecological change (e.g., Landsat 7 SLC failure 2003, Landsat 5 end 2011, Sentinel-2 start 2015).
items.fix_hint: Report sensor-specific contributions by year. Apply harmonized surface reflectance products (e.g., Roy et al. 2016 HLS). Test for discontinuities in FSI time series at known sensor transition dates. Report within-year variance when multiple sensors overlap.
items.mcc_rationale: GEE pipeline documented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51 and Estado_Conocimiento_S50, but no Q1 citation for Landsat 5/7/8 ↔ Sentinel-2 harmonization method (e.g., Roy et al. 2016 transform coefficients).

items.title: Ninhue structural zero excluded without contamination analysis
items.evidence: Ninhue registered zero cases (structural zero)
items.mechanism: Ninhue exclusion inflates effect sizes if commune differs systematically; inclusion without structural-zero modeling may dilute signal. No rationale or sensitivity provided.
items.fix_hint: Report Ninhue's forest cover, population, and FSI distribution vs other communes. Conduct sensitivity analysis including Ninhue (1) as observed zero-inflation component, (2) as standard observation, (3) excluded (current). Report how exclusion affects θ and IRR estimates.
items.mcc_rationale: Ninhue structural zero exclusion mentioned in Estado_Conocimiento_S50 and S53_blindaje, but no quantitative contamination analysis (spatial spillover test, residual autocorrelation check) nor manuscript sentence integrated.

items.title: Negative-binomial vs zero-inflated model selection undocumented
items.evidence: The negative-binomial family (Cameron & Trivedi 2005) accommodates rare-event overdispersion in zoonotic surveillance data. ... 97.7% recorded zero cases
items.mechanism: With 97.7% zeros, the data-generating process may include both structural zeros (communes with no Chusquea habitat) and count zeros (random low incidence). Standard negative-binomial assumes all zeros arise from the same process. Zero-inflated negative-binomial or hurdle models could better distinguish these mechanisms, potentially improving calibration and discrimination.
items.fix_hint: Report Vuong test comparing NB2 vs ZINB. Present zero-inflation parameter estimates for communes. Test whether structural zeros correlate with forest cover or elevation. Report AIC/BIC comparison and rootogram to assess zero-count fit.
items.mcc_rationale: Cameron & Trivedi 2005 cited for NB2 family in GLMM_NB2_Final.md, 97.7% zero inflation documented, but no AIC/BIC table comparing NB2 vs zero-inflated alternatives (ZINB, hurdle) integrated in manuscript.

items.title: Commune spatial scale arbitrary relative to rodent home range
items.evidence: comuna–month panel in Ñuble Region, south-central Chile (21 comunas, population ≈487,866)
items.mechanism: Oligoryzomys longicaudatus home range is ~0.5-2 hectares (Silva-Rodríguez et al. 2008), while communes span 100-2000+ km². Aggregating cases and FSI to administrative units 6-7 orders of magnitude larger than the biological process scale introduces ecological fallacy and may dilute fine-scale exposure-outcome associations. Optimal spatial grain is undetermined.
items.fix_hint: Report sensitivity using alternative spatial grains: (1) 1×1 km grid cells, (2) 5×5 km grid, (3) commune. Compare IRR effect sizes and CIs across scales. Discuss Modified Areal Unit Problem (MAUP). Report intra-commune variance in FSI to quantify aggregation-induced information loss.
items.mcc_rationale: Commune scale (21 comunas) documented in Estado_Conocimiento_S50 and Manuscrito_v5, but no Q1 citation justifying spatial resolution relative to O. longicaudatus home-range (~0.5–2 ha) nor sensitivity analysis at finer scales.

items.title: Socioeconomic and occupational confounders uncontrolled
items.evidence: β_log_pop = +0.614 (p = 0.002) ... Andes-virus hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome is classified among neglected tropical diseases linked to poverty and ecosystem disturbance
items.mechanism: Population size proxies both denominator (exposure opportunity) and urbanization/poverty. Commune-level poverty, agricultural employment, and housing quality are established HCPS risk factors (Prist et al. 2021; Abramson et al. 2003) that correlate with both forest proximity (FSI exposure) and case detection, creating confounding bias. The population coefficient may reflect unmeasured socioeconomic factors rather than pure exposure opportunity.
items.fix_hint: Include commune-level covariates: Social Priority Index, % agricultural employment, median household income, % rural housing. Test for residual confounding using E-values for SES-case association. Conduct stratified analysis by urban vs rural communes.
items.mcc_rationale: Population effect β=+0.614 (p=0.002) documented in GLMM_NB2_Final.md and poverty linkage mentioned, but no Q1 citation for socioeconomic/occupational confounder control nor explicit adjustment calculation in manuscript.

items.title: Seasonality modeled as fixed factor without cyclic constraint
items.evidence: β_season · season_f_t ... season_f a 4-level categorical factor
items.mechanism: Four-level categorical seasonality imposes discontinuities at quarter boundaries (Dec→Jan, Mar→Apr, etc.) and uses 3 degrees of freedom without enforcing temporal smoothness. Cyclic splines or Fourier terms would more realistically capture gradual seasonal transitions and reduce parameter burden, potentially improving out-of-sample prediction.
items.fix_hint: Compare current 4-level factor against (1) cyclic cubic spline with 4 df, (2) Fourier terms (2 harmonics), using out-of-sample log score. Report AIC and residual temporal autocorrelation. Visualize fitted seasonal curves to assess boundary artifacts.
items.mcc_rationale: All prior snippets are empty strings. No Q1 citation, calculation, or manuscript sentence found regarding cyclic/non-cyclic seasonality factor constraint.

items.title: Companion study overlap with undisclosed shared authorship
items.evidence: A companion clinical analysis of 34 HCPS patients treated at Hospital Clínico Herminda Martín, Chillán, 2010–2025 has been submitted separately (Contreras et al., Revista Chilena de Infectología)
items.mechanism: Companion manuscripts with overlapping data sources, study periods, and single-author bylines risk fragmenting findings for publication maximization (salami slicing). While ICMJE criteria are cited, absence of co-author disclosure prevents assessment of intellectual independence. Reviewers cannot verify that case-fatality and case-count data are presented optimally in each venue.
items.fix_hint: Disclose all authors on companion manuscript. Provide table mapping which data elements (cases, CFR, time periods) appear in each manuscript. Confirm both journals received pre-submission notification per ICMJE guidelines. Archive both manuscripts together at Zenodo to document complementarity.
items.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence confirms dual-manuscript strategy (project_dual_manuscript_factory_S44 multiple hits), companion clinical submission explicitly documented, and manuscript text integrated in Paper_EID_Final.md.
